why: After 'to je' (this is), the noun stays in the nominative case.
The subject of 'být' (to be) takes the nominative case.
After 'to jsou' (these are), the noun stays in the nominative case (plural).
'Do' (to/into) always takes the genitive case.
'Z' (from) always takes the genitive case.
'Od' (from a person) always takes the genitive case.
'Bez' (without) always takes the genitive case.
'U' (at/near) always takes the genitive case.
'Vedle' (next to) always takes the genitive case.
'Kolem' (around) always takes the genitive case.
'Podle' (according to) always takes the genitive case.
'Do' (to/back to) always takes the genitive case.
'Bez' (without) always takes the genitive case.
'K' (towards/to a person's place) always takes the dative case.
The indirect object of 'dát' (to give) takes the dative case — the person receiving something.
The indirect object of 'říct' (to tell) takes the dative case — the person being told.
'Pomáhat' (to help) governs the dative case.
'Věřit' (to believe/trust) governs the dative case.
The direct object of 'volat' (to call/phone someone) takes the accusative case.
'K' (towards) always takes the dative case when expressing movement toward something.
'K' (towards/to someone's place) always takes the dative case.
The direct object of 'vidět' (to see) takes the accusative case.
The direct object of 'mít' (to have) takes the accusative case.
The direct object of 'chtít' (to want) takes the accusative case.

'Na' (to/onto) takes the accusative case when expressing direction or movement toward a destination. Note: many places (e.g. kino, divadlo, škola) take 'do' + genitive instead — only an open/elevated/institutional subset takes 'na'.
The direct object of 'kupovat' (to buy) takes the accusative case.
The direct object of 'číst' (to read) takes the accusative case.
The direct object of 'hledat' (to look for) takes the accusative case.
'Pro' (for) always takes the accusative case.
'Přes' (across) always takes the accusative case.
The direct object of 'znát' (to know someone) takes the accusative case.
The direct object of 'mít rád' (to like) takes the accusative case.
When directly addressing someone, Czech uses the vocative case.
When calling out to someone to get their attention, Czech uses the vocative case. With professional titles, use 'paní'/'pane' first (see paní/pane templates).
When thanking someone by name, Czech uses the vocative case for direct address.
When greeting someone by name, Czech uses the vocative case for direct address. With professional titles, prefix 'paní'/'pane' (e.g. 'Dobrý den, paní profesorko.').
Czech requires the honorific 'paní' before a female professional title in formal address. The title itself stays in the vocative.
Czech requires the honorific 'pane' (vocative of 'pán') before a male professional title in formal address. The title itself stays in the vocative.
'V' (in) takes the locative case when expressing location inside an enclosed place. Open/institutional places (pošta, nádraží, pláž…) take 'na' instead.
'Na' (on/at) takes the locative case. Some places (pošta, nádraží, letiště, pláž…) take 'na'; enclosed/institutional places (kino, divadlo, škola, obchod) take 'v' instead.
'O' takes the locative case when expressing the topic of discussion (about).
'V' (in) takes the locative case for enclosed workplaces. Some workplaces (pošta, úřad, nádraží) take 'na' instead.
'Na' (on) takes the locative case when expressing the surface where someone is sitting.
'Po' (after) takes the locative case when expressing time sequence.
'Při' (during) always takes the locative case.
'V' (in) takes the locative case when expressing location.

'Na' (on) takes the locative case when expressing location on a surface.
